verses.text: Solomon gathers Israel's elders to bring up the ark to the temple, and they offer sacrifices beyond number. The whole nation assembles around the presence of God. The climax of all the building is not the gold but the coming of the LORD to dwell among His people.

Solomon gathers Israel's elders to bring up the ark to the temple, and they offer sacrifices beyond number. The whole nation assembles around the presence of God. The climax of all the building is not the gold but the coming of the LORD to dwell among His people.

Solomon gathers Israel's elders to bring up the ark to the temple, and they offer sacrifices beyond number. The whole nation assembles around the presence of God. The climax of all the building is not the gold but the coming of the LORD to dwell among His people.

The priests set the ark in the most holy place beneath the cherubim's wings; in it are only the two tables of the law from Horeb. God's word lies at the heart of His dwelling. His presence and His commandments belong together at the very center of true worship.

The priests set the ark in the most holy place beneath the cherubim's wings; in it are only the two tables of the law from Horeb. God's word lies at the heart of His dwelling. His presence and His commandments belong together at the very center of true worship.

As the priests come out, a cloud fills the house, so they cannot stand to minister, 'for the glory of the LORD had filled the house.' God takes possession of His temple. The goal of all the labour was His presence; without that, the most golden house is empty.

As the priests come out, a cloud fills the house, so they cannot stand to minister, 'for the glory of the LORD had filled the house.' God takes possession of His temple. The goal of all the labour was His presence; without that, the most golden house is empty.

Solomon declares, 'The LORD said that he would dwell in the thick darkness; I have surely built thee an house to dwell in.' The unseen God condescends to meet His people in a place. Mystery and nearness meet: He dwells in darkness, yet truly with us.

Solomon declares, 'The LORD said that he would dwell in the thick darkness; I have surely built thee an house to dwell in.' The unseen God condescends to meet His people in a place. Mystery and nearness meet: He dwells in darkness, yet truly with us.

Solomon turns and blesses the people, recalling that since the Exodus God chose no city for a house until He chose David. The temple is the fruit of God's long, patient choosing. What we enjoy today rests on purposes God set in motion generations ago.

Solomon turns and blesses the people, recalling that since the Exodus God chose no city for a house until He chose David. The temple is the fruit of God's long, patient choosing. What we enjoy today rests on purposes God set in motion generations ago.

Solomon turns and blesses the people, recalling that since the Exodus God chose no city for a house until He chose David. The temple is the fruit of God's long, patient choosing. What we enjoy today rests on purposes God set in motion generations ago.

He tells how David longed to build, but God reserved the work for his son, and now it is done — 'the LORD hath performed his word.' The promise made to the father is kept in the son. God's word never fails; what He speaks in one generation He fulfills in the next.

He tells how David longed to build, but God reserved the work for his son, and now it is done — 'the LORD hath performed his word.' The promise made to the father is kept in the son. God's word never fails; what He speaks in one generation He fulfills in the next.

He tells how David longed to build, but God reserved the work for his son, and now it is done — 'the LORD hath performed his word.' The promise made to the father is kept in the son. God's word never fails; what He speaks in one generation He fulfills in the next.

Solomon spreads his hands to heaven: 'LORD God of Israel, there is no God like thee,' who keeps covenant and mercy with His servants. He pleads that God's promise to David stand. True prayer begins by exalting God's uniqueness and resting on His covenant faithfulness.

Solomon spreads his hands to heaven: 'LORD God of Israel, there is no God like thee,' who keeps covenant and mercy with His servants. He pleads that God's promise to David stand. True prayer begins by exalting God's uniqueness and resting on His covenant faithfulness.

'But will God indeed dwell on the earth? behold, the heaven cannot contain thee; how much less this house.' Yet Solomon asks that God's eyes be open to it, to hear and forgive. The infinite God stoops to hear; the house is not His cage but His meeting place with sinners.

'But will God indeed dwell on the earth? behold, the heaven cannot contain thee; how much less this house.' Yet Solomon asks that God's eyes be open to it, to hear and forgive. The infinite God stoops to hear; the house is not His cage but His meeting place with sinners.

Solomon prays that God would judge between men who swear at His altar, and that when Israel is defeated for sin yet repents, God would forgive and bring them home. Defeat is meant to drive His people back to Him; He hears the cry of the chastened and restores them.

Solomon prays that God would judge between men who swear at His altar, and that when Israel is defeated for sin yet repents, God would forgive and bring them home. Defeat is meant to drive His people back to Him; He hears the cry of the chastened and restores them.

When drought or famine comes because of sin, Solomon asks that God hear the repentant and 'teach them the good way.' Affliction is God's summons to return. He sends hard providences not to crush but to turn His people back to the path of life.

When drought or famine comes because of sin, Solomon asks that God hear the repentant and 'teach them the good way.' Affliction is God's summons to return. He sends hard providences not to crush but to turn His people back to the path of life.

When drought or famine comes because of sin, Solomon asks that God hear the repentant and 'teach them the good way.' Affliction is God's summons to return. He sends hard providences not to crush but to turn His people back to the path of life.

Whatever the plague, when a man knows 'the plague of his own heart' and spreads his hands toward the house, Solomon asks God to hear, forgive, and render to each according to his ways, 'for thou only knowest the hearts.' God deals with us by the heart He alone can see.

Whatever the plague, when a man knows 'the plague of his own heart' and spreads his hands toward the house, Solomon asks God to hear, forgive, and render to each according to his ways, 'for thou only knowest the hearts.' God deals with us by the heart He alone can see.

Whatever the plague, when a man knows 'the plague of his own heart' and spreads his hands toward the house, Solomon asks God to hear, forgive, and render to each according to his ways, 'for thou only knowest the hearts.' God deals with us by the heart He alone can see.

Solomon prays even for the stranger from a far country who comes for God's great name, that God would hear him, 'that all people of the earth may know thy name.' The house was to be a place of prayer for the nations. God's heart reaches beyond Israel to all who seek Him.

Solomon prays even for the stranger from a far country who comes for God's great name, that God would hear him, 'that all people of the earth may know thy name.' The house was to be a place of prayer for the nations. God's heart reaches beyond Israel to all who seek Him.

When God's people go out to battle at His sending and pray toward this place, Solomon asks the LORD to maintain their cause. Prayer is the true weapon of God's people. Even in war, their hope is not in the sword but in the God to whom they turn.

When God's people go out to battle at His sending and pray toward this place, Solomon asks the LORD to maintain their cause. Prayer is the true weapon of God's people. Even in war, their hope is not in the sword but in the God to whom they turn.

Foreseeing exile for sin, Solomon prays that if the captives 'bethink themselves' and turn with all their heart, praying toward their land, God would hear from heaven. No distance is too far for repentance to reach Him; even from a foreign prison, the turning heart finds God listening.

He asks God to forgive His people and grant them mercy before their captors, for they are His inheritance brought out of Egypt. The ground of pardon is that they are God's own, redeemed by Him. Our hope of mercy rests not on our worth but on belonging to Him.

Rising from his knees, Solomon blesses the assembly: 'Blessed be the LORD, that hath given rest unto his people... there hath not failed one word of all his good promise.' Not one promise fell to the ground. The God who kept every word to Israel will keep every word to us.

Rising from his knees, Solomon blesses the assembly: 'Blessed be the LORD, that hath given rest unto his people... there hath not failed one word of all his good promise.' Not one promise fell to the ground. The God who kept every word to Israel will keep every word to us.

He prays God will be with them as with their fathers, incline their hearts to Himself, and that 'your heart be perfect with the LORD.' The goal of all worship is a whole heart toward God. Outward temples mean nothing without an inward devotion that is undivided.

The king and all Israel offer a vast sacrifice and keep the feast, then go home 'joyful and glad of heart' for all God's goodness. The dedication ends in worship and joy. When God's presence comes and His promises stand, the fitting response is glad, grateful celebration.
